question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 192km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (37kg each) and 6 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 110km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 54min, 3rd: 69min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 220km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (53kg each). The boat can travel at 42km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 78km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 85 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 62 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 62min, 2nd: 78min, 3rd: 94min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 189km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (73kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.

From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 72km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 60 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 60min, 2nd: 77min, 3rd: 94min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 14 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 156km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 85min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 219km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (48kg each) and 6 units of construction material (72kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 117km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 86 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 79min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 212km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 11 units of construction material (56kg each) and 5 units of relief supply box (41kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 187km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 79min, 3rd: 95min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (44kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (24kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 120km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 69min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (60kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (63kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 96km waypoint, 7 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 87 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 78min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of medical kit (6kg each) and 5 units of construction material (59kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 202km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (52kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (70kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.

From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 29%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 187km waypoint, 6 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 63 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 63min, 2nd: 77min, 3rd: 91min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (73kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 9 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 7 units of construction material (68kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 96km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 78min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (11kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (65kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 35km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 27%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 104km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 88min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
solution:

  - River-trip simulation with cargo, congestion, and rests; final numeric answer lives only in [STEP 3].
[STEP 1] Given (initial conditions & rules)
  - Total distance=211km, boat still-water speed=36km/h, departure=9:00 AM

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 200km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 8 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 140km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 83 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 82min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 204km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 14 units of construction material (75kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (65kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 65km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 58 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 58min, 2nd: 73min, 3rd: 88min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (65kg each) and 8 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 192km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 223km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 12 units of construction material (71kg each) and 8 units of relief supply box (52kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 23%.

From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 27%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 144km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 69min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 195km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (66kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (16kg each). The boat can travel at 33km/h in still water.

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 214km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (62kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (7kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 32km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 187km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 35 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 35min, 2nd: 49min, 3rd: 63min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 9 units of relief supply box (55kg each) and 4 units of construction material (63kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 19km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.

From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 140km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 85min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

[STEP 0] Problem meta

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (13kg each) and 3 units of construction material (58kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 104km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 73min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 206km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of medical kit (12kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (46kg each). The boat can travel at 35km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 25% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.

From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 192km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 58min, 3rd: 75min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 203km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (70kg each) and 8 units of construction material (77kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 30km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 36km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of water barrel (21kg each) and 7 units of construction material (61kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 78km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 78min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 209km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (55kg each) and 7 units of construction material (57kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 29%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 88km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 213km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of medical kit (9kg each) and 6 units of construction material (58kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.

From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 26%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 78km waypoint, 3 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 64min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 224km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 17 units of construction material (73kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (23kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.

From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 24%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 96km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 92 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 68min, 3rd: 85min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River scout Lee is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 193km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (57kg each) and 4 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 29%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 117km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 64min, 3rd: 79min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 213km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 7 units of water barrel (20kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 21km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 55km waypoint, 6 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 88 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 43 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 43min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 71min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (14kg each) and 8 units of construction material (55kg each). The boat can travel at 43km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 165km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 82 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 66min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 225km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 18 units of construction material (78kg each) and 6 units of relief supply box (32kg each). The boat can travel at 36km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 26km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 182km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 221km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (49kg each) and 8 units of construction material (72kg each). The boat can travel at 39km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 26%.

From 8:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 156km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 54 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 54min, 2nd: 69min, 3rd: 84min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 202km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (31kg each) and 8 units of construction material (53kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 130km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 80 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 72min, 3rd: 87min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 234km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (43kg each) and 7 units of construction material (60kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 34km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 117km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 77 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 57min, 3rd: 74min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Waterway officer Jang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 10 units of construction material (58kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (5kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 29km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.

From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 211km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of relief supply box (70kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (14kg each). The boat can travel at 29km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 20km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 99km waypoint, 4 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 78 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 49 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 49min, 2nd: 66min, 3rd: 83min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 15 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 191km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 8 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 8 units of construction material (74kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 20km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 24% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 117km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 55 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 55min, 2nd: 71min, 3rd: 87min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (45kg each). The boat can travel at 27km/h in still water.

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 212km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 18 units of relief supply box (61kg each) and 8 units of construction material (80kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

From 9:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 22%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 88km waypoint, 2 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 79 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 62min, 3rd: 76min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Channel guide Oh is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 210km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 6 units of medical kit (9kg each). The boat can travel at 28km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 31km is Zone A (speed limit: 23km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 19% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 25%.

From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 78km waypoint, 8 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 48 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 15 minutes (1st: 48min, 2nd: 63min, 3rd: 78min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 215km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of construction material (69kg each) and 7 units of relief supply box (54kg each). The boat can travel at 34km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 28km is Zone A (speed limit: 28km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 17% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 28%.

From 11:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 21%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 130km waypoint, 3 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 13km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 73min, 3rd: 89min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 11 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 207km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (62kg each) and 7 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 30km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 22%.

From 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 154km waypoint, 3 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 75 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 52 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 52min, 2nd: 69min, 3rd: 86min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Vessel operator Cho is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 200km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 9 units of construction material (71kg each) and 4 units of relief supply box (44kg each). The boat can travel at 30km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 24km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 21km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 20% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 9:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 77km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 86 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 57 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 16 minutes (1st: 57min, 2nd: 73min, 3rd: 89min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 7 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 216km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 16 units of construction material (59kg each) and 5 units of medical kit (10kg each). The boat can travel at 31km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 22% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.

From 8:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 25%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 198km waypoint, 8 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 84 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 41 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 41min, 2nd: 55min, 3rd: 69min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 194km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 4 units of construction material (66kg each). The boat can travel at 37km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 6km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 26km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 18% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 27%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 150km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 81 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 10km). The base rest duration is 51 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 51min, 2nd: 65min, 3rd: 79min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
River engineer Hwang is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 199km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 17 units of relief supply box (58kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 40km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 19km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 21% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 29%.

From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 132km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 91 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 11km). The base rest duration is 50 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 50min, 2nd: 67min, 3rd: 84min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 213km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of relief supply box (47kg each) and 3 units of construction material (69kg each). The boat can travel at 32km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 33km is Zone A (speed limit: 22km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 20km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (900kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 21%.

From 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 23%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 180km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 76 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 14 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 64min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 5 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 219km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 16 units of relief supply box (61kg each) and 4 units of construction material (75kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.

The river has a current of 5km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).

The first 27km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 18km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.

If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1000kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 23% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 24%.

From 11:00 AM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limit in Zone B is additionally reduced by 28%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.

At the 192km waypoint, 1 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 86 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 12km). The base rest duration is 58 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 17 minutes (1st: 58min, 2nd: 75min, 3rd: 92min, ...).

Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
